A couple of things I notice in this file that better to be fixed now:
1. The `self.action_dist_class_fn` seems to be an implementation detail, so it should be private. The actual public api is `self.get_action_dist_cls ` which is all readers of this class should care about. 
2. The following error message is using .format() syntax which is inconsistent and verbose compared to f-string that we are trying to standardize across rllib.
```
UnsupportedSpaceException(
                        "Action space has multiple dimensions "
                        "{}. ".format(action_space.shape)
                        + "Consider reshaping this into a single dimension, "
                        "using a custom action distribution, "
                        "using a Tuple action space, or the multi-agent API."
                    )
```
3. The deterministic flag of `get_dist_cls_from_action_space`  seems redundant after we added `to_deterministic()` api inside the distribution classes. All we need to do now is to get the distribution based on the action space and then call `to_deterministic()` on it.

I think the "and" case is actually more complex. So say:
- Downstream asset monitors upstreams A and B
- A materializes partitions 1 and 2, in that order
- B materializes partitions 3, 2, 1 in that order
The cursor can't actually be updated unless if the set of partitions in the first N materializations are the same for upstreams A and B. So in this case, until we get a partition 3 materialization in asset A, we can't advance the cursor for B (and thus can't advance the cursor for A)

Will do. For context, Go coverage analysis is a form of codegen. It rewrites the Go source code being examined to include explicit references to "coverage variables" which contain the statement counts for coverage analysis. The test main generated for a Go test binary has to explicitly reference the coverage variables generated by this codegen and register them with the coverage runtime.

Currently, I have the names of the coverage variables output as a result of building the package under test. Those variables could probably be output as a result of just running the code generation. In the end, it probably doesn't matter since the package under test will end up being built any way and Pants will cache that build so making it a dependency of the test main (as needs to happen) won't result in duplicative work.

You have several `await Get`s in this rule, which are all blocking / synchronous. It'd be better to parallelize via `await MultiGet`.

https://www.pantsbuild.org/docs/rules-api-tips#tip-use-multiget-for-increased-concurrency

It often gets too overwhelming to have that many `Get`s inlined, so you can do something like:

```python
download_coursier_get = Get(DownloadedExternalTool, ExternalToolRequest, ...)
wrapper_script_get = Get(Digest, CreateDigest, ...)
downloaded_coursier, wrapper_script = await MultiGet(download_coursier_get, wrapper_script_get)
```

There is (atleast) one situation where this patching doesn't work. We have the module `torchvision.models.alexnet` that defines the function `alexnet`, which is then imported into the `torchvision.models` namespace. Thus, after the import is complete, we can no longer access the module through `torchvision.models.alexnet`.

This is an anti-pattern and should be avoided. Given that everything we expose everything under `torchvision.models`, I suggest making all the other modules private. This avoids this naming issue and gives us the freedom to change the underlying structure however we want without needing to keep BC.

So the output here looks like this:

```json
[
  {
    "URL": "https://tensorboard.dev/experiment/abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv/",
    "Name": "[No Name]",
    "Description": "[No Description]",
    "Id": "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv",
    "Created": "2019-10-25 07:22:30",
    "Updated": "2019-10-25 07:22:34",
    "Scalars": "3208",
    "Runs": "8",
    "Tags": "4"
  }
]
```

There are a few problems with this:

 1. The keys are the same as the human-readable form, and in particular
    are capitalized and (after #3464) may have whitespace.
 2. Numeric values like `runs` and `tags` are strings, not ints.
 3. Name and description have dummy values instead of proper `""`s.
 4. The output is slurped and not streamed.

On (1): After #3464, this will have an extra `"Binary object bytes"`
key. But this is pretty unidiomatic. JSON keys do not typically have
whitespace; you can’t use such a key in a `jq` dot-selector:

```
$ printf '{"one": 1, "two three": 23}' | jq '.one'
1
$ printf '{"one": 1, "two three": 23}' | jq '.two three'
jq: error: syntax error, unexpected IDENT, expecting $end (Unix shell quoting issues?) at <top-level>, line 1:
.two three     
jq: 1 compile error
```

On (2): The integers are being converted to string for human
presentation, but should be left as ints in the JSON output for
programmatic manipulation.

On (3): Similar to (2). The dummy values are just to prevent confusing
human-readable output with a blank space. There’s no ambiguity in using
JSON `""`.

On (4): I see that you considered a streaming response but didn’t like
that it was “less easy to parse programmatically”. Is it? You can
convert a streaming response to a buffered response by simply piping
through `jq -s`, but you can’t go the other way around without waiting
for the whole buffer. And as long as we make sure to print one record
per line, people using tools other than JQ should be able to deal with
it handily (`for line in file: json.loads(line)` works in Python, e.g.).

One way to resolve these could be to change `data` from a 2-column table
to a 4-column table with human readable name, JSON key, raw value, and
formatter used when displaying to humans: e.g.,

```
            data = [
                ("Name", "name", experiment.name, lambda x: x or "[No Name]"),
                ("Description", "description", experiment.description, lambda x: x or "[No Description]"),
                ("Id", "id", experiment.experiment_id, str),
                ("Created", "created", util.format_time(experiment.create_time), str),
                ("Updated", "updated", util.format_time(experiment.update_time), str),
                ("Scalars", "scalars", experiment.num_scalars, str),
                ("Runs", "runs", experiment.num_runs, str),
                ("Tags", "tags", experiment.num_tags, str),
            ]
```

and project out the appropriate fields in the JSON and non-JSON cases;
of course feel free to pick a different implementation if you prefer.

The need for `n+1` is indeed not exactly straight forward: As you say, `ns[dim]` is the number of contextual regions for each dim (to calculate graylevel mappings). The number of blocks to process separately for interpolation is however different from this number in the general case, as becomes clear from [this](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Adaptive_histogram_equalization&oldid=936814673#Efficient_computation_by_interpolation) wikipedia schematic (which I also linked in a code comment to clarify). Depending on how many pixels are padded at the end of the dim (to have the contextual regions well defined), `ns[dim]` blocks can be sufficient to process all pixels within the shape of the input image. Otherwise `ns[dim]+1` blocks are needed.

The 2d version in `master` always processes `ns[dim]+1` blocks, however thanks to your comment I went through it and realised the last block can in many cases be dropped (as it only processes pixels which will be discarded during unpadding. Thanks :) Let me know if you agree and what you think!

The code paths here are asymmetric and intertwined (as the need for multiple `if self.json:` shows).  This is in part because the json path collects all the entries first and then prints them at the end (as noted in the PR description), but the non-json version is streaming.

First let's make this print exactly the same json output, except in a streaming manner:

```
if self.json:
    print "["
for experiment in gen:
    ...
    if self.json:
        print(textwrap.indent(json.dumps(data, indent=2), prefix="  "))
    ...
if self.json:
    print "]"
```

Next, to be more persnickety and to make it easier to add other formats, you could factor out the format-specific parts by creating an abstract `Formatter` class with methods `prefix()`, `format_experiment(data)`, and `suffix()`.  Then implement those for text and json, respectively, and pass the appropriate Formatter in to `_ListIntent` (or at least, pick the right one in the constructor).

By making the Formatter methods return strings (as opposed to `print(...)`ing as a side effect) you can make the choice of destination (stdout vs file, etc.) an orthogonal concern.

@TMVector, It seems we need to use `op_type=ModelProto.graph.name`. Because the names of `NodeProto`s must be unique in a single graph, if we do `op_type=Graph + op_name=ModelProto.graph.name`, we can only call the inference once in a training graph.

Let's consider calling `InferenceGraph` twice as an example. If we use `op_type=Graph + op_name=ModelProto.graph.name`, we will have
```
X --> Node(name="MyInferenceGraphName", op_type="Graph") -->Y
X_1 --> Node(name="MyInferenceGraphName", op_type="Graph") -->Y_1
```
which breaks the uniqueness of operator names. Maybe it's not a problem but we need another discussion to see if everyone agrees on it.

In contrast, if we do `op_type=ModelProto.graph.name`, we can have
```
X --> Node(name="CallMyGraph", op_type="MyInferenceGraphName") -->Y
X_1 --> Node(name="CallMyGraphAgain", op_type="MyInferenceGraphName") -->Y_1
```

Is this an error you hit on Linux? It looks like on Linux this is `errno.EMFILE`, which would make sense, since `EMFILE` means "you hit the limit on the number of file descriptors that a single process is allowed to have open". (There's also the confusingly-similar `ENFILE`, which means "you hit some kind of systemwide limit on the number of file descriptors".) So at the least, we should change this to `errno.EMFILE` instead of having a magic integer in the source code :-). And probably add `ENFILE` as an option too. Or maybe simplify to

```
except OSError as exc:
    assert exc.errno in (errno.EMFILE, errno.ENFILE)
    break
```

BTW, a handy trick: if you have some errno number and you want to figure out what the error code is, you can do:

```python
>>> import errno
>>> errno.errorcode[24]
'EMFILE'
```

If there are multiple variables then we won't be able to match them to the correct annotated values. For example, we will get `string -> ['~"\'.*?\'"i']` in the action sequence twice for the query below and we won't know which is the actor and which is the director.

```
        "sql": [
            "SELECT COUNT( DISTINCT ( ACTORalias0.NAME ) ) FROM ACTOR AS ACTORalias0 , CAST AS CASTalias0 , DIRECTED_BY AS DIRECTED_BYalias0 , DIRECTOR AS DIRECTORalias0 , MOVIE AS MOVIEalias0 WHERE ACTORalias0.NATIONALITY = \"actor_nationality0\" AND CASTalias0.AID = ACTORalias0.AID AND DIRECTORalias0.DID = DIRECTED_BYalias0.DID AND DIRECTORalias0.NAME = \"director_name0\" AND MOVIEalias0.MID = CASTalias0.MSID AND MOVIEalias0.MID = DIRECTED_BYalias0.MSID ;"
        ],
        "variables": [
            {
                "example": "Iran",
                "location": "both",
                "name": "actor_nationality0",
                "type": "actor_nationality"
            },
            {
                "example": "Jim Jarmusch",
                "location": "both",
                "name": "director_name0",
                "type": "director_name"
            }
        ]
```

So, as you can see in the testing code, we want to have a connection with just 1 pool, then block that pool so that we don't have any available pools left, after that call any method that makes a call to execute. When no pool is left, the execute is a coroutine instead of future and we have confirmed that in commit a62d010 where we get the expected error (before adding ensure_future). However, because we never release the pool, it will wait a long time waiting for a pool to be available (verified in commit 10a8fd6, before adding timeout). After adding timeout, we expect the patch to work successfully and then fail from the timeout while waiting for a free pool.

Ideal way would be to have 2 different tasks where the first one would block the pool, second one will make this requests and only after that the first task will release the block. However, I'm not sure how to manage that case asynchronously in tests, so, timeout was the second best idea. I got the code used for blocking the pool (with (await pool)) and timeout from the aioredis repository's testing where they tested pools for aioredis 1.3.

This is related to `Buffer` definition, please refer to src/ray/common/buffer.h.

For example, by default when you initialize a `LocalMemoryBuffer` with a data pointer and a length, it just assigns the pointer and length, but doesn't copy the data content, this is for performance reasons, but in this case the buffer cannot ensure data validity, it instead relies the lifetime passed in data pointer. 

This is fine for most cases - for example when you put an object into store, you create a temporary RayObject and don't want to do an extra copy. But in some cases you do want to always hold a valid data - for example, memory store uses `RayObject` to represent objects, in this case you actually want the object data to remain valid after user puts it into store.

> Yeah, what _does_ `publicID` actually mean? Is it just alternate (archaic?) terminology for the graph identifier, i which case we should probably just purge it, or does it have some other meaning?

From the docstring: “The publicID argument is for specifying the logical URI for the case that it's different from the physical source URI”.

It's necessary for correct serialization, e.g. reading the file `examples/foaf.rdf` and parsing it as `data` without a publicID results in URLs being rewritten as the file source, e.g.
```py
rdflib.term.URIRef('file:///home/gjh/dev/rdflib/images/timbl-image-by-Coz-cropped.jpg')
```
whereas using the publicID of `http://dig.csail.mit.edu/2008/webdav/timbl/foaf.rdf` results in the correct serialisation:
```py
rdflib.term.URIRef('http://dig.csail.mit.edu/2008/webdav/timbl/images/timbl-image-by-Coz-cropped.jpg')
```
Just using the CSAIL URL as identifier doesn't result in correct serialization so it does appear that `publicID` has a valid and continued role.

Well, someone else needs to give this guidance I think - you won't like my opinion. My opinion is that strict is preferred - its the only true lock. When you admit sources in addition you admit untested paths. So you've locked, but you have no clue if:
1. The locked sdist, when built a year later, even though it matches the fingerprint at lock time, doesn't unleash havoc with malicious code that does not match the wheel contents.
2. The locked sdist has different Requires-Dist / Requires-Python. This is much better than 1 - you won't suffer an attack, but you will suffer a resolve failure since there will be dependencies missing from the lock.

When you go further and admit universal, its the same problem en-masse.

If you want me to write down all this and are OK with the opinion, I can do that - but none of this is specific to Pex or even Python, this is true conceptually for any locking system where there is more than 1 distribution.

@bradenmacdonald, it's `run_aggregator_service`. `reaggregate_course` didn't need more than a few minutes.

> I thought you said you added a lock to prevent overlapping runs of it, so how did you use concurrent workers to speed it up?

Oh, I now remember the specific use case we had in mind here.
The reason we implemented this is because we have this specified in Ansible as:
```yaml
EDXAPP_ADDITIONAL_CRON_JOBS:
  - name: "run aggregator service"
    user: "{{ edxapp_user }}"
    job: ". {{ edxapp_app_dir }}/edxapp_env && {{ edxapp_venv_bin }}/python {{ edxapp_code_dir }}/manage.py lms run_aggregator_service --settings={{ edxapp_settings }} >/dev/null 2>&1"
    minute: "*/30"
    hour: "*"
    day: "*"
  - name: "run aggregator cleanup"
    user: "{{ edxapp_user }}"
    job: ". {{ edxapp_app_dir }}/edxapp_env && {{ edxapp_venv_bin }}/python {{ edxapp_code_dir }}/manage.py lms run_aggregator_cleanup --settings={{ edxapp_settings }} >/dev/null 2>&1"
    minute: "15"
    hour: "*"
    day: "*"
```
Therefore this job is present on each instance in the auto scaling group. This would fire all of them simultaneously, so we are using Redis cache locks here (which use atomic operations) to be sure that only one instance is able to run this.

The lock is only for scheduling Celery tasks, not for running them. We didn't create the locks to protect ourselves from the infinite loops, but this can be an issue if you run the `run_aggregator_service` too often while having tons of new block completions. This is not a very likely scenario, but it's technically possible. This could potentially be mitigated by marking `StaleCompletion`s as `resolved` once they are scheduled for the Celery task (or by adding a `scheduled` field to this model), but I'm not fully aware whether this would not cause any side effects.

`expires > ?` will realistically never hold true as `expires` in the `set(...)` method is the number of seconds from now when the key expires.

Example: 

```python
# This sets a key to be expired 300 seconds from now (i.e. 5 minutes)
set("my-key", b"my-value", 300)`
```

This means that in the `get(...)` method, the comparison that is made is `300 > 1691232468` (the latter is the current epoch time gotten from `time.time()`) which will not be true, even if the key was just set.

The change you need to make, is in the `set(...)` method set the `expires` column to `int(time.time()) + expires`. That will make the `get(...)` method work properly.

I don't see much of a use case for projects wanting to specify a different security model for each package it installs on a _package_ basis, but rather on a task or flow basis. I _think_ it's more likely to be "install everything with X model" or, for regression, "install everything with X model and then run install_beta with Y model".

I have a suggestion here:
How about instead of this, we add a method like 
```
    /**
     * This method indicates whether a given neighbor is in the router table.
     *
     * @note This method checks whether the `aNeighbor` instance itself is an entry from the router table.
     *
     * @param[in] aNeighbor   A reference to a neighbor.
     *
     * @returns TRUE if the neighbor is present in the router table, FALSE otherwise.
     *
     */
    bool ContainsNeighbor(const Neighbor &aNeighbor) const;
```

Two reason for this:
- I think it will help simplify the code where we use this to check.
- The fact that `RouterTable` uses an array to store router entries is a private info in the `RouterTable`. Publicly, the `RouterTable` provides methods to search/find a entry and an `Iterator` to go through the entries (which does now show what the internal data structure is). The whole notion of router table "index" (which is only applicable when we know we have an array) is not part of public APIs of `RouterTable`.  Using `ContainsNeighbor` would be more generic (it allows us in future to consider changing internal data structure used to store routers from array).

```
      (16, 3, 400, 300)  |  2354.8  |  316.1  |    299.5
```

Unfortunately `add_done_callback` on asyncio (which Tornado using on python 3) made it another way.
It was mentioned on Tornado docs (http://www.tornadoweb.org/en/stable/releases/v5.0.0.html#backwards-compatibility-notes):

> The timing of callbacks scheduled with Future.add_done_callback has changed. tornado.concurrent.future_add_done_callback can be used to make the behavior more like older versions of Tornado (but not identical). Some of these changes are also present in the Python 2 version of tornado.concurrent.Future to minimize the difference between Python 2 and 3.

And we can see it from asyncio docs (https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-future.html#asyncio.Future.add_done_callback):

> If the Future is already done when this method is called, the callback is scheduled with loop.call_soon().

I was trying to avoid using that because we're testing that. I did a similar thing with computing blue/green. My thought was that the real "strength" of a wired name, even though it has weaknesses, is that it specifies an anchor that other tests will judge consistency against. If it uses those other tools, it's at risk of them being wrong. And maybe that argues for failing on the throttling or using a more low-tech other throttling. It might make sense to call that other function and compare the result to make sure we agree we're talking to the right page. It seems like a hard thing for the get_health_page thing to get wrong, but I'm ignoring that and thinking more in terms of what are the consequences if it got the health page for the wrong server, and it seems like the consequence is that a lot of integration tests are done to the wrong resource, which could put that resource at some risk.

In general, I think testing code is not like regular code in that sometimes things are inelegant, redundant, differing in syntax and that can be good because otherwise if you're too regular you may not trip up some subtle difference. The core value in algebra as a concept, whether high school algebra, abstract algebra, or the kind of weird programming algebra we do is that no matter which way you go to get an answer, you want to get the same answer. But in programming that's an ideal more than a reality, so testing goodness by going different routes can sometimes be a virtue in itself because there is additional confidence in something tested several ways.

First, I'm worried about the statefulness this introduces to the `chaindb` instance which is a shared resource.  This code is very likely to become problematic within the `trinity` context since there will be multiple processes/coroutines interacting with the `chaindb` and thus if this value changes mid-stream we'll get weird bugs.

I'd like to explore how we can get away from this pattern.  

Properties we need from the API:

- the `chaindb` *needs* to know the proper `BlockClass` to use.  
- Ideally we avoid having to specify a new `ChainDB` class for every fork since it's likely that the internal logic won't need to be changed.

And last, we've been wanting to move away from the inheritance based model that we are currently using in the current `eth.vm` namespace in favor of something that is composition based.

> Forgive 

### Idea A: pass `block_class` as a parameter into the `ChainDB.__init__` class.

* Benefits:
  - compositional
* Cons
  - requires the `chaindb` to exist at the fork level rather than the chain level, meaning it will need to be re-instantiated for each fork.  This may result in some larger refactors.

### Idea B: pass `block_class` into any method that needs it.

* Benefits
  - makes the `ChainDB` agnostic about the rich encoding classes.
* Cons
  - requires much more explicit code

### Idea C: create an `Encoder` class which acts as the translator between the `ChainDB` and the `StateMachine`.  Each `StateMachine` would require an `Encoder` class like this:

```python
BlockClass = TypeVar("BlockClass", bound=BaseBlock)

class Encoder(Generic[BlockClass]):
    @classmethod
    def get_block_class(cls) -> BlockClass:
        ...
    def encode_block(block: BlockClass) -> bytes:
        ...
    def decode_block(raw_block: bytes) -> BlockClass:
        ...
```

Then each `StateMachine` would need to be told which `Encoder` class to use which ideally isn't done with inheritance but a class variable seems like the ideal way to do this...

And then the `ChainDB` would need to be given the `Encoder` instance via some API, potentially by passing it into any method that needs it.

### Closing thoughts

I think I'm leaning towards either B or C, with both being different approaches to the same concept.  I think passing in the data to `chaindb.__init__` may prove problematic as it would move the `ChainDB` from being a `Chain` level object down to being a `StateMachine` level object, thus I think it may be appropriate to require each method which needs this data to require it as a parameter.  Note that option C allows a smooth transition from `RLP` to `SSZ` since the `Encoder` class abstracts away any encoding details.

It took me quiet a while to understand the above comment.
Base on my understanding, I'm trying to summarize the comment and make it easier to understand for future readers. 

-------

This map is used to track all the unreleased dummy objects for this actor. The map key is the dummy object ID, and the map value is the number of actor handles that depends on this dummy object. When the map value decreases to 0, the dummy object is safe to release from the object manager.

An actor handle depends on a dummy object when its next unfinished task depends on the dummy object. For a given dummy object (say D), there could be 2 types of such actor handles:
1) The actor handle (say H) on which D's creating task (say T) was submitted. If T's next task hasn't finished yet, H still depends on D.
2) Any handles that were forked from H after T finished, and before T's next task finishes.  Such handles depend on H until their first tasks finish.

In the past, `resource_group` is an optional parameter.

1. If not provided, we will query it. If provided, we will use it directly to get registry. Since registry name is globally unique, resource group for a registry is also unique.
2. If the correct `resource_group` is provided, the behavior is the same as not provided.
3. If the wrong `resource_group` is provided, we error out since the resource couldn't be found.

As of this PR, `resource_group` will always be queried. So (1) and (2) will be the same. For (3), the new behavior is that the registry can still be found, rather than error out.

```py
    def acquire_token_silent_with_error(
            self,
            ...):
        """Acquire an access token for given account, without user interaction.
        ...
        :return:
            - A dict containing no "error" key,
              and typically contains an "access_token" key,
              if cache lookup succeeded.
            - None when there is simply no token in the cache.
            - A dict containing an "error" key, when token refresh failed.
        """
```

It's possible that the DHCP6 client is stopped while it's still soliciting. That is the DHCP6 prefix is removed right after the client solicited.
Currently, the issue is revealed because the BR (which is DHCP6 server & client & owner of the DHCP6 prefix) learns the Network Data containing the DHCP6 prefix from another Thread device after reset, and removes the DHCP6 prefix immediately because of Network Data inconsistency. 
Such issue can also occur by a small chance when a DHCP6 client starts soliciting and DHCP6 server removes the prefix at the same time.

Stopping the trickle timer when stopping DHCP6 client resolves this issue.

Agreed on not updating the macro, but I think the cleaner place to fix this is the `python_requirement_library` target itself. This avoids adding yet more complexity to the already complex module_mapper.py.

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/824a15daa9f633554b5544ae2c59405601e20e1c/src/python/pants/backend/python/target_types.py#L586-L593

You'll override the `compute_value()` method like this: https://www.pantsbuild.org/docs/target-api-new-fields#adding-custom-validation. But rather than custom validation, you will mix-in the global default with something like

```python
provided_mapping = super().compute_value(raw_value, address=address) or {}
return {**DEFAULT_MODULE_MAPPING, **provided_mapping}
```

Two notes here:
1. `super().compute_value()` returns `dict | None`, and we need to handle that `None` case.
2. We want the user provided mapping to override the global default, the order of that dict unpacking matters.

--

While you're at it, please set `value: FrozenDict[str, Tuple[str, ...]]`, which will override the default of 

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/824a15daa9f633554b5544ae2c59405601e20e1c/src/python/pants/engine/target.py#L1178-L1179

We know it will never be `None` anymore because you're guaranteed to mix in the default dictionary. This will allow you in the `module_mapper.py` code to remove this `or {}  # type: ignore`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/bd81ccaa96b343983d9bd62c7a514390a37e0cb4/src/python/pants/backend/python/dependency_inference/module_mapper.py#L185

You mean what happens when some nodes call MPI_Finalize(), but some nodes call MPI_Abort()? It appears that in that case, the nodes that MPI_Finalize() don't fail and can complete their execution, but the nodes that call MPI_Abort() will exit with non-zero exit code. So none of them hang. In another scenario, if during the phase when the nodes are still communicating with each other (like when loading data or training is still in progress), some nodes want to keep communicating while some others call MPI_Abort(), all processes exit with non-zero error code. Previously all processes used to hang in this scenario because the failing nodes were calling MPI_Finalize().

I tend to prefer code that doesn't have early return statements. It makes it much easier to copy-paste segments into IPython when I'm doing interactive development. I also like having a single return as the last line of a function, so you know that whatever code path is taken must always populate that specific return variable. However, this is only a minor preference, and I'm willing to make this modification. 

However, I would prefer to keep the existing implementation without using `expanduser('~')`. The expanduser function is actually defined in different files in the stdlib depending on your OS. I think its nicer to have a single code path that shows behavior on all system. Also this code cuts out a lot of the extra work that `expanduser('~')` would have to do because we aren't replacing anything in a string we are only finding the path to the user's home directory. While using expanduser is more concise I think the current implementation demystifies what expanduser is actually doing under the hood. My vote is to keep this, but I'll yield to your opinion if you feel strongly about it.

The dtor can get called from within the worker thread because of shared_ptr's reference counting:

device holds subdevice_model holds processing_block_model-list holds filter-ptr holds worker thread running in the bg
device goes away
~device calls ~subdevice_model calls ~processing_block_model calls ~filter-ptr

Here is where it gets interesting:

We don't want to destroy the filter until we're in a good place in the worker thread, because the worker thread is dependent on data that sits in there. So we have the worker thread lock the filter-ptr. But this means that, once the shared_ptr is released, the ~filter gets called and we're in the worker thread.

So the idea of `extra_logs` is that the message will appear in the same `[INFO]` as the "wrote dist to path/" message. I think this is important - when you run `./pants package ::`, you'll get lots of `[INFO]` messages, and it's consistent if each `[INFO]` corresponds to exactly one asset. I'm concerned a user will not realize that the extra logs correspond to a prior `[INFO]`.

This is especially a concern if you're returning multiple assets in one `BuiltPackage`, and the log info applies to each asset. That won't be clear; you'd have multiple `[INFO] wrote to dist/`, and only show the extra info once at the end, making it look like it only applies to the last asset.

Ah, you've stumbled upon precisely the part that makes the interpreter difficult to implement, but once implemented makes the API pleasant and powerful.

Look at the `field_name` values of the `project_property` calls. They are going to be `name`, `color`, and `name`, almost certainly in that order. Here's what's happening:
- First call (`name`): this is to get the value of the `name @filter(...)` part.
- Second call (`color`): this is to get a value for the `value: ["%color"]` tagged parameter inside the filter.
- Third call (`name`): this is to perform `name @output(...)`.

You correctly point out that there are 10 animals, and it would be a valid execution of the interpreter if it called `project_property` for each used property for each animal (30 calls in total). But it clearly doesn't do that.

To see why, recall the interpreter's desirable optimality ("anything you can do, I can do at least as well") property. This property doesn't hold if we can show an optimization that is prevented by the interpreter API's design, or by the interpreter library's implementation.

Making anything more than the 3 calls I described above does prevent a very desirable optimization: user-specified batching. Imagine `name` for animals 1-4 is fetched in one `project_property` call, and for animals 5-10 in another `project_property` call. This prevents the user from batching 10 (or 8, or all, or `random.randint()`) animals at a time — no implementation of the `InterpreterAdapter` API is able to batch over input data it is unable to receive as input, since the `data_contexts` input generator will run out.

So optimality requires that *all* the relevant `data_contexts` are delivered in a single call to `project_property` (or any of the other two functions that receive generators as input). That means that the set of function *calls* in the trace is purely a function of the input query, and does not depend on the data.

Taking a step back, *this* is why the interpreter is so challenging to build: because it has to dynamically construct a chain of generators in *just the right way* that fulfills the target query without knowing how the 4-method `InterpreterAdapter` API is going to be implemented, and by extension guaranteeing correct results while not knowing the exact order of operations between those generators beyond "data is created before it is consumed."

We consistently have the raft suite pass locally, but we noticed that actually the key errors that are logged in the Travis builds that failed are also logged when running the tests against raft even though they don't result in tests failing.

Looking into that further we realized that when we refactored out the slot check into a method we added a bug -- the slot name isn't present in the slot dict, so a key error was being thrown on basically every invocation (if `ignore_slots` had any config). Our tests passed though with raft because that error was being swallowed, and all of the tests were about slots being ignored. On the other hand etcd3 and kubernetes appear to stop doing anything when that key error occurs, which results in the failing test since Patroni never actually gets to the code that configured the state of the server.

We're pushing a fix that handles this issue plus updates the tests to guarantee it.

I explained during the 1-1 why I want a dedicated release pipeline, but I write it here for the record. So the main reason is not to have a build of the changelog file, which is more a bonus. The main reason is that the release pipeline does not fulfill the same purpose than the advanced one, even if (for now) it does effectively execute the same actions.

As the release pipeline will provide the artifacts for the release, I want it to be clearly separated from the advanced pipeline in the Azure DevOps UI and so the release process. It is supposed to contains deployable artifacts, while the advanced pipeline will most of the time be the result of nightly tests, or tests on demand for the `test-*` branches. In the future, it would also fit naturally in so-called Azure DevOps release pipeline (the CD part), that would typically be triggered by a specific CI pipeline (here, `release.yml`) and the publication of its pipeline artifacts.

Also the risk of divergence between the pipelines is largely mitigated by the use of job templates, as it is done here.

In term of release workflow, it is true that state of the art on CI/CD is more in favor of define the deploy of new release based on a deployable branch than a deployable tag. Typically a release flow is triggered by a push to the deployable branch (invoking the CD at this time), and a tag is added at the end of the flow to mark that deployable artifacts have been effectively deployed. We should certainly go to that direction in the future.

So, in that aspect @bmw, considering that I remove the `*.x` branch trigger from `advanced.yml`, is it better:
* build `release.yml` on push to `*.x` branches
* build `release.yml` on tag push of type `v*`?

Either way I will apply your proposition and calculate the version of certbot from the sources, not from the tag or branch name that is been built.

The previous should catch the very small fraction of projects that have dumped `setup.py` but forgot to actually set the correct `build-system` section in the `pyproject.toml`; otherwise, for those have the former but are missing the latter, `build` (like all standards-conforming tools) assumes `setuptools.build_meta:__legacy__` (i.e. direct `setup.py` invocation), which _should_ provide close to identical behavior when requesting the metadata as the deprecated monkeypatching here (so either both will fail, or neither will). And if PEP 517 builds are used and `build-system` is properly configured, metadata retrieval should basically boil down to reading the `setup.cfg` or `pyproject.toml`, or at most executing the initial stages of the `setup.py` in a similar fashion as here.

That said, its not impossible there are cases that could trigger this; I'd be very eager to learn what they are, if so.

I haven't done so already as i didn't want to brake the backward compatibility and because smart_remove() checks the dumb_mode flag with each for loop tick (that's why it might be slightly slower). 1) leave it as it is
2) replace the remove() calls with smart_remove(dumb_mode=True) calls everywhere and get rid of remove() method
3) rename smart_remove() to remove() and replace the dumb_mode flag with smart_mode flag in the current implementation -  then we will have only remove(int) calls or remove(int, smart_mode=True) calls everywhere.

I added a comment paragraph to the code. This is mostly for testing purposes. Generally for tests we would like to start a trial as soon as its resources are ready without calling `step()` another time (as it will be a non-deterministic number of times we would have to call `step()`, which is bad for the current test setups). So we have to wait until the placement group is ready, so we can start the remote actor on it. 

The number of seconds we wait is given by `self._wait_for_pg`. However, if we only used that we would always wait for, say, 5 seconds each time we try to start a trial. This is bad though, because in some cases we actually do have limited resources and the trial cannot be started until other trials finished. This will then dramatically slow down result handling (and in our cases, test execution time). So we only want to wait when it is likely that we will have resources available soon. This is the case when a) we just staged the trial (which is usually at the start of the run, or after another trial finished), b) when another trial actually finished (if the trial is already staged, but the resources only become available now), or c) when there are no running trials (maybe a trial errored and we're just waiting for the resources for the final trial).

This is also the reason for the `trial_just_finished` variables below

Consider having a string column that is split into 2 virtual chunks:
```
a b c d | e f g h
        ^- virtual chunk split
```
"Virtual" means that there's a single buffer backing both of the chunks, the only difference is the offset attribute of each chunk. So basicly the buffers for the string column are:
```
data: 	  a b c d e f g h
offsets:  0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
validity: None
```
Chunking both of the buffers (offsets and the data) invalidates the offsets values for the second chunk:
```
data:    a b c d | e f g h
offsets: 0 1 2 3 | 4 5 6 7 8
                   ^- the offsets are incorrect for this chunk,
                      the possitions for the first 'e' string are
                      ``0:1``, not ``4:5``.
```
There're actually two ways to resolve invalidation:
1. Modify offsets values, so the values for each chunk always starts from 0:
    ```
    data:    a b c d | e f g h
    offsets: 0 1 2 3 | 0 1 2 3 4
                       ^- the offsets values are now correct, but that
                          required us to modify the values (and so make a copy)
    ```
2. Return the same data buffer for each chunk:
    ```
    data:    a b c d e f g h
    offsets: 0 1 2 3 | 4 5 6 7 8
                       ^- as the 'data' buffer is the same for each chunk, 
                          ``4:5`` is valid positions for strings in the second chunk,
		              but that makes a consumer to keep in memory the whole 'data' buffer
                          of the column for each chunk
    ```

Originally, I chosen the second option, but after explaining the approach in the comment I tend to the first way as the second one seems to eliminate all the benefits of chunking by making to read the whole column over and over again for each chunk.

What are other possible options here? Do you think I should change the implementation to align it to the first approach?

At the point where I left this comment, the generator has raised StopIteration. That means it's exhausted. An exception can't escape from a generator if there's anything the generator still might yield. These uses (close() after catching an exception raised out of the generator, including StopIteration) are superfluous.

The PR *also* uses close() to deal with cases where the generator has yielded too many times. That use is valid and useful. Note that close() still might not succeed in closing the generator, if the generator yields in a `finally` block, but that's a bug in the generator. In such cases close() will raise RuntimeError.

The external assembly support is not relevant for console app execution, because with console app execution the executing assembly will always reference the necessary resource package assemblies so it will always be OK to fall back on the on `AppDomain.CurrentDomain`. So strictly speaking, it didn't *need* to be in this PR.

But, in that vein, concurrent execution isn't necessary for console execution either.

In any scenario where you actually might *need* to make use of concurrent execution, it is important that the executing assembly be able to explicitly pass in assemblies to be used as resource package assemblies. Because:
1. Individual deployments might use different resource package assemblies
2. The executing assembly (like a theoretical Web API) might not explicitly reference those resource package assemblies itself so `AppDomain.CurrentDomain` wouldn't find them
3. Those assemblies might be loaded at runtime

At my company we will be using an internal Web API to automate deployment, and our pulumi deployments are abstracted as plugins. There's a plugin for an "application type" i.e. a plugin to deploy a static website, or one of our larger proprietary application types. So, the Web API is going to load the plugin to get the pulumi deployment code. It will not itself have the resource package assemblies in its `AppDomain.CurrentDomain` necessary for the deployment to function as needed.

And since it is possible for those assemblies to be loaded at runtime, Pulumi should be sure not to cache any of them too. Because if a reference is held to an `Assembly` or `Type` than that assembly can never be un-loaded.

Each `ProtocolColumn` chunk has its own underlying PyArrow table, multiple PyArrow tables may internally use a single shared buffer (as a result of virtual chunking), however modifying a single table does not propagate changes to the rest of them, it just triggers a copy for this particular chunk to do the modifications.

When it's required to do PyArrow table casting for a single chunk, it's required for the rest of them as well. So the idea is to make a one single cast for the whole column at once, rather than casting each chunk independently. The benefits are: less memory allocations, less memory fragmentations.

In deferred compute, the shape information is accessible as shape inference happens eagerly. If that value gets passed into a node attribute, then that attribute is frozen in the hybridized graph.

For example, suppose the query length is then used in normalization `x/length`, the operator invoked would be a broadcast scalar division, where the node attribute value of the divisor is from length, a python integer from `.shape[index]`. In that case, as the length changes, the division value would be wrong. To address it, one can use `.shape_array[index]` so that the op invoked is broadcast division and it's always the up-to-date length value that will be used.

The original code did not exit on `error == OT_ERROR_NONE`. And it would not be not correct behavior and not what we want.

Reordering this logic is needed to handle the scenario where `ddtrace-run` is used with `DD_LOGS_INJECTION=true DD_TRACE_DEBUG=true DD_CALL_BASIC_CONFIG=true` when there is a `ddtrace` logger. 

If a tracer object isn't available, it doesn't mean that `ddtrace.tracer` is available. If this is checked first, it can lead to an error that `ddtracer.tracer` doesn't exist:

```
E           File "/root/project/.riot/venv_py3103/bin/ddtrace-run", line 33, in <mod...eError: partially initialized module \'ddtrace\' has no attribute \'tracer\' (most likely due to a circular import)
```

This is because `tracer` doesn't exist under `ddtrace`:
```
dir(ddtrace)
['Pin', 'Span', '__builtins__', '__cached__', '__doc__', '__file__', '__loader__', '__name__', '__package__', '__path__', '__spec__', '_hooks', '_logger', '_monkey', 'config', 'configure_ddtrace_logger', 'constants', 'context', 'contrib', 'ext', 'filters', 'internal', 'patch', 'patch_all', 'pin', 'propagation', 'provider', 'sampler', 'settings', 'span', 'vendor', 'version']

```

Alternatively, I could also just check if `tracer` exists under `ddtrace` with `getattr`, but I'm not sure if there's a specific reason to check the tracer this way.

Its not explained, but backing up to the locally assumed domain specific knowledge of a venv and site-packages, `dst` is `site-packages` and `src` is some installed wheel chroot. Those typically look like:
```
ls -l ~/.pex/installed_wheels/23de6e1192493377da687afdbb2a2cf11e6d613a/Django-3.2.10-py3-none-any.whl/
total 12
drwxr-xr-x  2 jsirois jsirois 4096 Dec 13 10:26 bin
drwxr-xr-x 18 jsirois jsirois 4096 Dec 13 10:25 django
drwxr-xr-x  2 jsirois jsirois 4096 Dec 13 10:26 Django-3.2.10.dist-info
```
So when you symlink this into `site-packages` you need:
```
site-packages/
  django -> ...
  Django-3.2.10.dist-info -> ...
  foo_dist -> ...
  foo-dist-1.0.0.dist-info -> ...
```
  
So, basically, each `src` has more than one item that must be linked into one big flat `dst`. There are many `src`s.

I don't think we can prevent `ciso8601` from parsing the other formats it supports, and this is where the undefined behavior in the spec is useful. We can guarantee parsing works for the formats we want to support, and everything else is left up to the implementation or completely undefined. It communicates that it's not a breaking change if we decide in the future to switch from `ciso8601` to another library that parses other "extras" in addition to the formats we promise to support.

I really don't think there's value in implementing our own parser, and I really don't think we stand to gain anything by making our spec tighter than "here are the formats we definitely support, we don't lose precision within them, everything else is undefined or implementation-defined."

```
import modin.pandas as pd

# Build dataframe with ray engine.
os.environ["MODIN_ENGINE"] = "ray"
import ray
ray.init()

df = pd.DataFrame([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])

# Change to dask engine.
os.environ["MODIN_ENGINE"] = "dask"

df.sum()
```

this check bothers me a bit because it relies on the implementation of the "random" 128 bit trace id is generated. Since it uses the unix time stamp (a non-zero value) we're guaranteed that the generated 128bit id is > _MAX_UINT_64BITS. If implementation were to change and we truly generated random 128 bit trace ids then this check would not hold and this code would be broken.

I'd like to see a stronger variant that we use to establish that a span is using 128-bit ids. This could be done if we upfront stored the 128 trace id as a tag in the span on span creation and keep `span.trace_id` as just the 64 bit portion. Then here we would check the presence of that tag on the span.

1) In the context of integration tests using pytest, we want to silence this out. What is interesting is the output of Certbot, not the noise of the third parties, that we assume working correctly
2) In the context of `run_acme_server`, either to test Certbot or troubleshoot these third parties. In this case, we want the stdout of these processes to be displayed in the standard stdout, to be consumed as it is necessary

So, since `ACMEServer` is a context manager with everything needed for setup/teardown the context, I implemented a stdout alongside its lifecycle, to be used by all suprocesses. Its nature depends of a `stdout` parameter given to the `ACMEServer` constructor. If `True`, this stdout is sys.stdout, and we see the processes activity in the standard stdout. If `False`, we use a descriptor on `/dev/null` that is opened/closed accordingly in the context manager at entry and exit context.

For 1), `stdout` is False, for 2) it is `True`.

>This isn't the new execution model though--- just the legacy shim until we write the streaming executor. Hence, I'd like to avoid unnecessary execution detail changes that could cause behavior changes.

That makes sense, we can keep to the old implementation for the legacy shim and defer any changes until the streaming executor PR.

>Setting timeout=0 has historically caused issues with not returning all futures in a timely manner. I do not know if this issue has been fully addressed.

I don't think this would be ideal anyways, since we'd just be taking the relatively efficient Core Worker wait + a periodic signal check with an inefficient application-level spin loop. So agreed that this wouldn't be a good route.

> Setting no timeout is also problematic since then you need to set num_returns, breaking batching.

Hmm what do you mean by "batching" here?

If you mean batching of multiple "ready" returns, `num_returns` is set to 1 by default, so that kind of batching is already not happening here since when `num_returns=1`, we immediately return a single `ready` ref when it becomes available, right? Quick example demonstrating these unbatched semantics:
```
In [2]: o1 = ray.put(1)

In [3]: o2 = ray.put(2)

In [4]: done, not_done = ray.wait([o1, o2], fetch_local=True, timeout=0.1)

In [5]: done
Out[5]: [ObjectRef(00ffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff0100000002000000)]

In [6]: not_done
Out[6]: [ObjectRef(00ffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff0100000001000000)]
```

If you are talking about concurrent object pulls (which I don't think you are), that should have been fixed in Ray Core (with the bug + fix that you discovered) so `len(tasks)` is fetched instead of `num_returns`. https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/30724

In any case, unlike the stream executor which wants to immediately know that a task has finished in order to (potentially) launch new work (and should therefore have no timeout for its wait loop), the bulk executor only cares about updating the user-facing progress and stats reporting, so batching at a 100ms granularity is pretty reasonable. If that 100ms batching is the intention, then I think that we would want to specify `num_returns`:
```suggestion
            done, _ = ray.wait(tasks, num_returns=len(tasks), fetch_local=True, timeout=0.1)
```

But yeah, we can just keep the existing bulk execution wait loop behavior, since this is just a legacy shim.

There is a small risk associated with re-using the `EntryPoint` objects as returned by an old `importlib.metadata` implementation:

- If someone uses the `importlib_metadata.entry_points()`/`importlib_metadata.EntryPoints.select()` API, they expect the result to be a collection of `importlib_metadata.EntryPoint` objects.
- However, with the change what they will get is a collection of `Union[importlib_metadata.EntryPoint, importlib.metadata.EntryPoint]` which is not strictly API compatible with their expectations (and therefore may cause runtime errors).

If this risk is considered small enough, the change should be relatively safe.

Before removing it let me fight a bit for this modification: 

`.DS_Store` files are generated by mac computer all the time. Lucky me I didn’t execute the lib itself so there is just one but they can appear in any folder. All Mac IDE are hiding them from us meaning that before `git add` I simply don’t know where they are. If you refuse to add this line to the .gitignore you simply deny to all mac users the possibility to use `git add .` which can be a real pain when modifying many files. 

Let’s have a look at the `.gitignore` itself. it’s not a « commitizen » specific file but the github template for Python language with small customization. It includes: 
- IDE specific folders (.vscode)
- sphinx folders (docs/_build/)
- all the django related files
- translation files (.mo, .po) 
- scrappy stuff

Yep, thanks; I actually thought of that too in case any of our packages actually do use this value. In this case, it was never imported/exposed in `__init__` (or anywhere else public) to begin with, just in its own private internal-only module, so I didn't do that here. If for some reason we do want to, though, since its a constant in the top-level package namespace and wasn't here before, it would make sense to make it `VERSION_INFO`.

The issue with this (and equally, the old `version_info` -> `__version__` approach) versus just using `__version__` (which is what virtually all third party packages are likely to look for anyway, and has no real use internally) is that in the canonical PEP 440-normalized form, there isn't supposed to be a `.` between the last version component and alpha/beta/rc tags, only for  `devN`. This will get normalized in the canonical version in the package metadata, but is strongly discouraged and means the version stored in the package metadata, used by packaging tools and exposed via `importlib.metadata`, `pkg_resources`, etc will be inconsistent with the version in `__version__`. While this isn't a regression over the old approach, using `__version__` would free us from that limitation.

This is a tricky one, tbh. This is a TL:DR; of where we landed on this while writing the tutorial:

- A _code location_ is a thing unto itself, and exists even without a `Definitions` object. Code locations are defined by Dagster itself (with or without a `Definitions` object), but when a `Definitions` object *is* defined, the code location will use it to find the definitions it needs to be aware of.

- A `Definitions` object is a way to combine multiple definitions into a single mega-definition, which allows the definitions to be aware of each other. A `Definitions` object isn't analogous to a code location.

All of this to say I think the way we're thinking about this is you can add `Definitions` **to** a code location, but creating a `Definitions` object doesn't create a code location.

`count(f.X)` returns the number of valid (non-NA) elements in column X in each group. The `countna(f.X)` is supposed to return the number of invalid (NA) elements in column X in each group. The `count()` function without an argument returns the total number of elements in each group. Thus, the following invariant must hold:
```
countna(f.X) == count() - count(f.X)
```

I started trying to jot down an issue but I think it's best if you do it, I have some doubt if it is unconditionally a good idea. The '*' trick to require call sites to use keywords is a good idea in my book, in fact I'd require all methods to use keywords if it was up to me (like in Smalltalk) but what I have doubts about is sorting the required arguments. 

Sorting.. seems weird to me. Either the schema has order, in which case the schema authors can arrange the most aesthetically pleasing order of these positional arguments, or it does not have order. In case it does not have order, sorting makes arbitrary decisions that can feel ugly. If all args required keywords the caller could make the decision? 

Checking up how it works, perhaps this:

```
def foo_bar(*, a, b=None, c=None):
    pass

# foo_bar()
# TypeError: foo_bar() missing 1 required keyword-only argument: 'a

foo_bar(a=1)
```

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/1a1fea3453c6f329168f91c7cdfe95e043661256/torchvision/prototype/transforms/_auto_augment.py#L70-L71

But that is only a surface difference, since later in the kernel we do 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/1a1fea3453c6f329168f91c7cdfe95e043661256/torchvision/transforms/functional_tensor.py#L560

`fill.expand_as(image)` will broadcast to the right shape, since a singleton dimension is broadcastable with any number of channels.

Maybe I should add: if performance *really* matters, why do you have this code path in your loop?
Of course it's nice if it's fast.
But there is an opportunity cost to optimizations like this PR: things get more complicated and fragile.
Now we're in the realm of philosophy:
* Keep pybind11 lean and straightforward so that it is as good as it can be at wrapping with ease.
* Tell users: if you have a performance critical loop, put your data into a C++ array (that you can easily wrap) and have your performance critical loop in C++, taking Python completely out of that loop, literally.
So there are clear "performance really doesn't matter" and "performance really does matter" regimes where everybody is in agreement and happy, but a gray area in the middle, where it's not quite important enough for users to bite the bullet and code up the C++ array they'd need.
What's better globally over a long period of time for all users in aggregate?
* Having N bypasses like this in pybind11 to get more of that gray area?
* A leaner and less fragile pybind11?

Yes type checking is definitely more preferred. However, if you take a look at the actual implementations `TokenCredential` classes in Python Azure SDK, they actually implement them without an interface. There is no base class that is common amongst all types of `TokenCredential` s. This means that if ever a `TokenCredential` is implemented that does NOT implement `get_token` (since in Python, it is not enforced), our code will actually crash if we do not have the `hasattr` check. With `hasattr`, no matter how azure sdk implements the `TokenCredential`, as long as we have the function we need, we are okay.

Take a look  at [ManagedIdentityCredential](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/sdk/identity/azure-identity/azure/identity/_credentials/managed_identity.py#L42) as an example. Notice how it only extends `object`.

Also, Python azure sdk only defined [TokenCredential](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/sdk/core/azure-core/azure/core/credentials.py#L16) class for type checking (linting). There is no enforcement of actual implementations at all.

I saw in https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/string/byte/strerror that those functions will only work when `__STDC_LIB_EXT1__` is defined.

* (Dec 2017) https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47867130/stdc-lib-ext1-availability-in-gcc-and-clang

```
class SyncStatusFilter(FilterSet):

    member_of = ModelChoiceFilter(
        method="filter_member_of", queryset=Collection.objects.all()
    )

    def filter_member_of(self, queryset, name, value):
        return queryset.filter(Q(user__memberships__collection=value) | Q(user__facility=value))

    class Meta:
        model = UserSyncStatus
        fields = ["user", "member_of"]
```

```python
from modin.backends.base.doc_utils import doc_qc_method

def foo():
    pass

print(
    doc_qc_method(
        template="Perform {refer_to} operation.",
        refer_to="foo",
        refer_to_module_name="bar",
    )(foo).__doc__
)

# Output:
# Perform foo operation.
#
# Notes
# -----
# Please refer to ``modin.pandas.bar.foo`` for more information
# about parameters and output format.
```

When we use UUIDs for making directories, we don't have to worry about races, because UUID collisions are effectively impossible due to their length. When our directory names might be as short as 5 characters, collisions are very possible. This would probably manifest as the mkdir in make_public_dir failing because the directory already exists.

[`tempfile.mkdtemp`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html#tempfile.mkdtemp) is the Right Way to make a unique subdirectory. If for some reason we can't do that, I would unify the name-generation and directory-creation into one loop, using a failed mkdir as the test for the directory already existing.
